food_subgroup: Steamed milk desserts with glutinous rice cake

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for mochi double-layer milk custard. FOODON_03315091 (glutinous rice cake food product) is the closest parent covering the mochi component. The steamed milk custard (shuang pi nai) component has no dedicated FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKK (rice-based dessert) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for mochi or shuang pi nai in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mochi double-layer milk custard or its component shuang pi nai. SNOMED CT does not catalogue this specific Chinese dessert.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one bowl or cup as typically served in a Chinese dessert shop (tong sui pu).

nutrient_source.title: Shuang Pi Nai (Double Skin Milk) and Mochi (Glutinous Rice Cake) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Whole Milk (FDC ID 1097512), Egg White raw (FDC ID 1100183), Glutinous Rice cooked (FDC ID 168878), Sugar granulated (FDC ID 1701177)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mochi double-layer milk custard exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model: approximately 55% steamed milk custard (whole milk + egg white + sugar) and 45% mochi (glutinous rice flour + sugar + water). Proportions are based on typical shuang pi nai and mochi recipes documented in culinary literature. Values are cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy entries for each component. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — glutinous rice and steamed rice dessert analogues; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mochi double-layer milk custard. GI is estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous foods: glutinous rice products (GI 86–98 per Sydney GI Database) are moderated by the milk custard component (GI ~30–40 for dairy-based custard). The combined dish is approximately 45% glutinous rice mochi and 55% milk custard by weight. Weighted GI estimate: (0.45 × 90) + (0.55 × 43) ≈ 40.5 + 23.7 ≈ 64, rounded to 65. Glycemic load per 200 g serving: (65 × 27.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 35.75, rounded to 35.8. This is a medium-GI, high-GL food per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~48 mg per 100 g; ~96 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of mochi double-layer milk custard is relatively low at approximately 96 mg per standard serving, making it acceptable for hypertensive individuals when consumed occasionally, though portion control remains advisable given the sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~35.8) driven by glutinous rice mochi and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid mochi double-layer milk custard due to its high glycemic load (~35.8 per 200 g serving) from glutinous rice and added sugar, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited to no more than 100 g and paired with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whole milk (~2.6 g per 100 g) and moderate added sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mochi double-layer milk custard contains moderate saturated fat (~5.2 g per 200 g serving) from whole milk, and its high sugar content may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dessert infrequently and in small portions as part of an otherwise heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mochi Double-Layer Milk Custard (麻薯双皮奶, Má Shǔ Shuāng Pí Nǎi) is a Chinese dessert that combines two traditional preparations: shuang pi nai (double-skin steamed milk custard, a Cantonese specialty originating in Shunde, Guangdong) and mochi (glutinous rice cake). The dish is served in a bowl or cup and features a silky, lightly set steamed milk custard base made from fresh whole milk, egg whites, and sugar, topped or layered with soft, chewy mochi pieces made from glutinous rice flour. Some versions incorporate whole glutinous rice grains (such as those from Guangxi Bama, prized for their texture) alongside the mochi skin. The flavour profile is mildly sweet and milky, with a delicate eggy richness from the custard and a satisfying chewiness from the glutinous rice components. The texture contrast — smooth, trembling custard against springy, elastic mochi — is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), moderate protein from milk and egg white (~4.8 g per 100 g), and a significant carbohydrate load (~27.5 g per 100 g) predominantly from glutinous rice and sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), but the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is high (~35.8), making this dessert a concern for individuals managing blood glucose. It is a good source of calcium (~110 mg per 100 g) and riboflavin from the milk component. In Singapore, this dessert is served at Chinese dessert shops (糖水铺, tong sui pu) and is popular as an after-meal sweet or afternoon snack. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mochi double-layer milk custard exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component model using USDA SR Legacy entries for whole milk, egg white, glutinous rice, and sugar. GI is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice and dairy custard GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Beverages' (as in input) to 'Desserts' — mochi double-layer milk custard is a solid/semi-solid dessert, not a beverage. 2. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. All component data sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 3. GI estimated at 65 (medium) using weighted analogy from glutinous rice (GI ~90) and dairy custard (GI ~43) components; glycemic load per 200 g serving is high (~35.8). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in whole milk and glutinous rice components per USDA SR Legacy; value is confirmed zero by component analysis. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy milk fat profile. 6. Serving size set at 200 g based on typical Chinese dessert shop portion. 7. Hindi alias in aliases_by_language uses Devanagari script: मोची डबल-लेयर मिल्क कस्टर्ड — note: the flat aliases list contains the identical string. 8. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 and FoodEx2 A0CKK used as closest_parent matches; no exact codes exist for this dish. 9. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the component-level USDA SR Legacy estimation model used; these are not confirmed zero values.
